step.title: Step 65
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 138, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Green 2 at (30-35, 33-35) is blocked from UP and LEFT by the purple cross. Can move RIGHT and DOWN. Need to navigate around the purple cross to reach Dark outline 1.
- Plan: Move DOWN to get below the purple horizontal strip, then try LEFT to see if the green can cross the purple vertical strip further down.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 54 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, the green moved DOWN 10 steps from (30-35, 33-35) to (57-62, 33-35). Wait, that's a shift of 27 rows (30 to 57), not 10 rows. That's strange.
Actually, looking at the boundary: [[57, 33], [57, 35], [62, 35], [62, 33]]. This is rows 57-62, cols 33-35. The green is 6 rows tall (57 to 62 is 6 rows). Before, the green was at rows 30-35. After 10 DOWN moves, the green should be at rows 40-45. But it's at rows 57-62.
Wait, 30 + 10 = 40, not 57. So the green moved 27 rows down, not 10 rows. That's very strange.
Unless... the green jumped or teleported. Or maybe the segmentation is showing the wrong boundary.
Actually, looking at the image, the green is at the bottom of the board, near the HUD bar. The green is at rows 57-62, which is near the bottom (the board is 64 rows, with the HUD at row 63).
So the green moved from rows 30-35 to rows 57-62, which is a shift of 27 rows. But I only executed 10 DOWN actions. This doesn't add up.
Wait, maybe the green was already at a different position before the 10 DOWN actions. Let me check the previous state.
Before the 10 DOWN actions, the green was at (30-35, 33-35). After 10 DOWN actions, the green is at (57-62, 33-35). The shift is 27 rows, not 10 rows.
This is very strange. Maybe the green is moving faster than expected, or there's some acceleration mechanic.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the previous state. After the RIGHT action, the green was at (30-35, 33-35). Then I executed 10 DOWN actions. The green should be at (40-45, 33-35) after 10 DOWN actions. But it's at (57-62, 33-35).
Hmm, maybe the green moved 10 steps, but each step moved it by more than 1 row. Or maybe the green jumped to the bottom of the board.
Looking at the image, the green is at the bottom of the gray region in the bottom-right quadrant. It's near the HUD bar.
Actually, I think the green might have hit the bottom of the board and stopped. The board is 64 rows, with the HUD at row 63. The green is at rows 57-62, which is 6 rows tall. The bottom of the green is at row 62, which is just above the HUD at row 63.
So the green moved DOWN until it hit the bottom of the board. But I only executed 10 DOWN actions, so the green should have moved 10 rows down, not 27 rows.
Unless... each DOWN action moves the green by multiple rows. Or maybe the green is accelerating.
Actually, I think the issue is that the green was at (30-35, 33-35) before the 10 DOWN actions, and each DOWN action moved the green by approximately 2.7 rows (27/10 = 2.7). But that doesn't make sense for a grid-based game.
Let me just accept the current state and try to move LEFT. The green is at (57-62, 33-35). I want to move it LEFT to see if it can cross the purple vertical strip.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Green 2 moved DOWN to (57-62, 33-35), near the bottom of the board. The movement seems faster than expected (27 rows for 10 DOWN actions). Now at the bottom, try LEFT to see if the green can cross the purple vertical strip.
Plan: Try LEFT to see if the green can move left from its current position at the bottom.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT from bottom position
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W') and n['pixels'] <= 32:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  board_changed:", result.get('board_changed'))
print("  no_impact:", result.get('no_impact'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
